revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} | image = Nishina Shinmei Shrine-Honden.jpg | caption = Nishina Shinmei Shrine, Main Hall | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36|26|59.8|N|137|52|44.5|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = [[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = | website = {{Official|1=http://www.sinmeigu.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Ōmachi, Nagano|Ōmachi]], [[Nagano Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is the oldest extant example of ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', one of three architectural styles which were conceived before the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan]]. It predates in fact the more famous [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], which shares the style and has been since antiquity rebuilt every twenty years. It was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 </ref> It is most common in [[Mie prefecture]]. Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''[[honden]]'' is either 3x2 ''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="JAANUS">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Two of its structures, the {{nihongo|Main Hall|本殿|''[[honden]]''}} and the {{nihongo|Inner Gate|中門|''chūmon''}}, are listed as [[National Treasures of Japan]].
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{Cite web | last = The Agency for Cultural Affairs | script-title=ja:国指定文化財 データベース | work = Database of National Cultural Properties | accessdate = 2009-04-16 | date = 2008-11-01 | url = http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp | language = Japanese }}
==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]
[[Category-inline|Nishina-shinmeigū}}

This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 </ref> It is most common in [[Mie prefecture]]. Two of its structures, the {{nihongo|Main Hall|本殿|''[[honden]]''}} and the {{nihongo|Inner Gate|中門|''chūmon''}}, are listed as [[National Treasures of Japan]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Nishina-shinmeigū]][[en:Nishina Shinmei Shrine]][[fr:Nishina Shinmei-gū]][[hu:Nisina Sinmei Szentély]][[ja:仁科神明宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3342145]] Wikidata name: Nishina Shinmei Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} | image = Nishina Shinmei Shrine-Honden.jpg | caption = Nishina Shinmei Shrine, Main Hall | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36|26|59.8|N|137|52|44.5|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = [[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = | website = {{Official|1=http://www.sinmeigu.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Ōmachi, Nagano|Ōmachi]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is the oldest extant example of ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', one of three architectural styles which were conceived before the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]. It predates in fact the more famous [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], which shares the style and has been since antiquity rebuilt every twenty years. It was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 </ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie prefecture|Mie prefecture]]. Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''[[honden]]'' is either 3x2 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="JAANUS">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Two of its structures, the {{nihongo|Main Hall|本殿|''[[honden]]''}} and the {{nihongo|Inner Gate|中門|''chūmon''}}, are listed as [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]].
==Notes== <references/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nishina-shinmeigū]][[en:Nishina Shinmei Shrine]][[fr:Nishina Shinmei-gū]][[hu:Nisina Sinmei Szentély]][[ja:仁科神明宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3342145]] Wikidata name: Nishina Shinmei Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} | image = Nishina Shinmei Shrine-Honden.jpg | caption = Nishina Shinmei Shrine, Main Hall | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36|26|59.8|N|137|52|44.5|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = [[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = | website = {{Official|1=http://www.sinmeigu.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Ōmachi, Nagano|Ōmachi]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is the oldest extant example of ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', one of three architectural styles which were conceived before the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]. It predates in fact the more famous [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], which shares the style and has been since antiquity rebuilt every twenty years. It was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 </ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie prefecture|Mie prefecture]]. Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''[[honden]]'' is either 3x2 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="JAANUS">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Two of its structures, the {{nihongo|Main Hall|本殿|''[[honden]]''}} and the {{nihongo|Inner Gate|中門|''chūmon''}}, are listed as [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]].
==Notes== <references/>

Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 </ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie prefecture|Mie prefecture]]. Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''[[honden]]'' is either 3x2 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="JAANUS">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Two of its structures, the {{nihongo|Main Hall|本殿|''[[honden]]''}} and the {{nihongo|Inner Gate|中門|''chūmon''}}, are listed as [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]].

Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''[[honden]]'' is either 3x2 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="JAANUS">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Two of its structures, the {{nihongo|Main Hall|本殿|''[[honden]]''}} and the {{nihongo|Inner Gate|中門|''chūmon''}}, are listed as [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]].

The shrine is the oldest extant example of ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', one of three architectural styles which were conceived before the arrival of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]. It predates in fact the more famous [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], which shares the style and has been since antiquity rebuilt every twenty years. It was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 </ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie prefecture|Mie prefecture]]. Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''[[honden]]'' is either 3x2 ''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="JAANUS">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Two of its structures, the {{nihongo|Main Hall|本殿|''[[honden]]''}} and the {{nihongo|Inner Gate|中門|''chūmon''}}, are listed as [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]].

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nishina-shinmeigū]][[en:Nishina Shinmei Shrine]][[fr:Nishina Shinmei-gū]][[hu:Nisina Sinmei Szentély]][[ja:仁科神明宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nishina-shinmeigū]][[en:Nishina Shinmei Shrine]][[fr:Nishina Shinmei-gū]][[hu:Nisina Sinmei Szentély]][[ja:仁科神明宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nishina-shinmeigū]][[en:Nishina Shinmei Shrine]][[fr:Nishina Shinmei-gū]][[hu:Nisina Sinmei Szentély]][[ja:仁科神明宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3342145]] Wikidata name: Nishina Shinmei Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} | image = Nishina Shinmei Shrine-Honden.jpg | caption = Nishina Shinmei Shrine, Main Hall | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36|26|59.8|N|137|52|44.5|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = [[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = | website = {{Official|1=http://www.sinmeigu.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Ōmachi, Nagano|en|Ōmachi, Nagano|WD=Q385375|lt=Ōmachi}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The shrine is the oldest extant example of ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', one of three architectural styles which were conceived before the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}}. It predates in fact the more famous [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], which shares the style and has been since antiquity rebuilt every twenty years. It was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 </ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie prefecture|Mie prefecture]]. Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''[[honden]]'' is either 3x2 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|WD=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi}}'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="JAANUS">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Two of its structures, the {{nihongo|Main Hall|本殿|''[[honden]]''}} and the {{nihongo|Inner Gate|中門|''chūmon''}}, are listed as {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)}}.
==Notes== <references/>

It was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 </ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie prefecture|Mie prefecture]]. Two of its structures, the {{nihongo|Main Hall|本殿|''[[honden]]''}} and the {{nihongo|Inner Gate|中門|''chūmon''}}, are listed as {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)}}.

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} | image = Nishina Shinmei Shrine-Honden.jpg | caption = Nishina Shinmei Shrine, Main Hall | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36|26|59.8|N|137|52|44.5|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = [[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = | website = {{Official|1=http://www.sinmeigu.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Ōmachi, Nagano|en|Ōmachi, Nagano|WD=Q385375|lt=Ōmachi}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} | image = Nishina Shinmei Shrine-Honden.jpg | caption = Nishina Shinmei Shrine, Main Hall | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36|26|59.8|N|137|52|44.5|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = [[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = | website = {{Official|1=http://www.sinmeigu.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Ōmachi, Nagano|en|Ōmachi, Nagano|WD=Q385375|lt=Ōmachi|1=Ōmachi, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The shrine is the oldest extant example of ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', one of three architectural styles which were conceived before the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}}. It predates in fact the more famous [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], which shares the style and has been since antiquity rebuilt every twenty years. It was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 </ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie prefecture|Mie prefecture]]. Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''[[honden]]'' is either 3x2 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}}'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|WD=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi|1=Hirairi}}'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi|1=Chigi (architecture)}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi|1=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="JAANUS">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Two of its structures, the {{nihongo|Main Hall|本殿|''[[honden]]''}} and the {{nihongo|Inner Gate|中門|''chūmon''}}, are listed as {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}.

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} | image = Nishina Shinmei Shrine-Honden.jpg | caption = Nishina Shinmei Shrine, Main Hall | map_type = Japan Nagano Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|36|26|59.8|N|137|52|44.5|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = [[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = | website = {{Official|1=http://www.sinmeigu.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Nishina Shinmei Shrine'''|仁科神明宮|Nishina Shinmeigū}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Ōmachi, Nagano|en|Ōmachi, Nagano|WD=Q385375|lt=Ōmachi|1=Ōmachi, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The shrine is the oldest extant example of ''[[shinmei-zukuri]]'', one of three architectural styles which were conceived before the arrival of {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}}. It predates in fact the more famous [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], which shares the style and has been since antiquity rebuilt every twenty years. It was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 </ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie prefecture|Mie prefecture]]. Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''[[honden]]'' is either 3x2 ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}}'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|WD=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi|1=Hirairi}}'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi|1=Chigi (architecture)}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi|1=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="JAANUS">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Two of its structures, the {{nihongo|Main Hall|本殿|''[[honden]]''}} and the {{nihongo|Inner Gate|中門|''chūmon''}}, are listed as {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}.
